perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees not to disclose or provide the Product, documentation, or any related information (including the Product features or the results of use or testing) to any third party or use the Product for any purpose other than as provided in this Agreement. However, Recipient may disclose confidential information in accordance with judicial or other governmental order, provided Recipient shall give MS reasonable written notice prior to such disclosure and shall comply with any applicable protective order or equivalent.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees not to disclose or provide the Product, documentation, or any related information (including the Product features or the results of use or testing) to any third party or use the Product for any purpose other than as provided in this Agreement. However, Recipient may disclose confidential information in accordance with judicial or other governmental order, provided Recipient shall give MS verbal notice prior to such disclosure and need not comply with any applicable protective order or equivalent.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes the requirement for written notice to verbal notice and removes the obligation to comply with protective orders, conflicting with standard legal practices for protecting confidential information during legal proceedings.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 26(c) allows courts to issue protective orders to protect parties from annoyance, embarrassment, oppression, or undue burden or expense related to discovery. By stating the Recipient need not comply with any applicable protective order, the contract contradicts this rule, which provides the basis for protective orders related to confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. Read the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure (PDF) Related Downloads Federal Rules of Civil Procedure 431.61 KB Return to top Your browser does not support Javascript. Your browser does not support Javascript.
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts standard legal practices, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation. Specifically, it removes the obligation to comply with applicable protective orders, which is directly addressed in the Law Explanation referencing FRCP 26(c). While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly mention protective orders, it broadly covers 'General Provisions Governing Discovery,' and Snippet 2 describes the purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure as securing a 'just' determination. Removing the requirement to comply with protective orders directly undermines the ability to protect confidential information, contradicting the 'just' determination. Therefore, the modification clearly contradicts the spirit and intent of civil procedure rules.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the complete and exclusive agreement between MS and Recipient with respect to the subject matter hereof, and supersedes all prior oral or written understandings, communications or agreements not specifically incorporated herein. This Agreement may not be modified except in a writing duly signed by an authorized representative of MS and Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes the complete and exclusive agreement between MS and Recipient with respect to the subject matter hereof, and supersedes all prior oral or written understandings, communications or agreements not specifically incorporated herein. This Agreement may be modified through mutual agreement, either verbally or in writing.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the contract to allow verbal modifications contradicts the Statute of Frauds, which requires certain contracts, including those lasting over a year, to be in writing.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text allows for verbal modifications of the agreement, which contradicts the Statute of Frauds, specifically RCW 19.36.010 in Washington, which requires certain agreements, including those not performable within one year, to be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. Since this agreement's term is indefinite, verbal modifications could render certain aspects unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.36.010: Contracts, etc., void unless in writing. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.36 > Section 19.36.010 Beginning of Chapter << 19.36.010 >> 19.36.020 PDF RCW 19.36.010 Contracts, etc., void unless in writing. In the following cases, specified in this section, any agreement, contract, and promise shall be void, unless such agreement, contract, or promise, or some note or memorandum thereof, be in writing, and signed by the party to be charged therewith, or by some person thereunto by him or her lawfully authorized, that is to say: (1) Every agreement that by its terms is not to be performed in one year from the making thereof; (2) every special promise to answer for the debt, default, or misdoings of another person; (3) every agreement, promise, or undertaking made upon consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; (4) every special promise made by an executor or administrator to answer damages out of his or her own estate; (5) an agreement authorizing or employing an agent or broker to sell or purchase real estate for compensation or a commission. [ 2011 c 336 s 540 ; 1905 c 58 s 1 ; RRS s 5825. Prior: Code 1881 s 2325; 1863 p 412 s 2 ; 1860 p 298 s 2 ; 1854 p 403 s 2 .]
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows for verbal modifications, which directly contradicts the Statute of Frauds, as outlined in RCW 19.36.010. The original text required modifications to be in writing, signed by authorized representatives. The changed text allows verbal modifications, making the agreement unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds for agreements not performable within one year. RCW 19.36.010(1) states that any agreement not to be performed within one year must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Washington and Recipient further consents to jurisdiction by the state and federal courts sitting in the State of Washington.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Washington, except in cases of intellectual property disputes, where Martian law shall apply. Recipient further consents to jurisdiction by the state and federal courts sitting in the State of Washington.
perturbation.explanation: The modification introduces an absurd and unenforceable clause stating that Martian law applies to intellectual property disputes, which is contradictory to established legal principles and jurisdictional norms.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Supremacy Clause of the US Constitution
perturbation.law_explanation: The Supremacy Clause (Article VI, Clause 2) establishes that the Constitution, federal laws, and treaties are the supreme law of the land. Applying "Martian law" directly contradicts this, as it undermines the established legal framework of the United States and Washington State.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Article VI | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Article VI Primary tabs All debts contracted and engagements entered into, before the adoption of this Constitution, shall be as valid against the United States under this Constitution, as under the Confederation. This Constitution, and the laws of the United States which shall be made in pursuance thereof; and all treaties made, or which shall be made, under the authority of the United States, shall be the supreme law of the land; and the judges in every state shall be bound thereby, anything in the Constitution or laws of any State to the contrary notwithstanding. The Senators and Representatives before mentioned, and the members of the several state legislatures, and all executive and judicial officers, both of the United States and of the several states, shall be bound by oath or affirmation, to support this Constitution; but no religious test shall ever be required as a qualification to any office or public trust under the United States. ‹ Article V up Article VII › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a clause applying "Martian law" to intellectual property disputes, which directly contradicts the Supremacy Clause of the U.S. Constitution, as explained by the LLM. Scraped Law Snippet 2 explicitly states that the Constitution and federal laws are the supreme law of the land, and judges in every state are bound thereby, anything in the Constitution or laws of any State to the contrary notwithstanding. Applying Martian law would undermine the established legal framework and render the agreement non-compliant and unenforceable within the U.S. legal system. The introduction of 'Martian law' constitutes a significant departure from established legal principles, creating a direct conflict with the supremacy of U.S. law.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the termination of this Agreement, Recipient shall promptly return to MS. or certify destruction of, all full or partial copies of the Product and related materials provided by MS. Section 6 shall survive termination or expiration of this Agreement with respect to any information that has not been made public by MS as of the commercial release of the Product.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the termination of this Agreement, Recipient may retain all full or partial copies of the Product and related materials provided by MS, and is not required to certify destruction. Section 6 shall not survive termination or expiration of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This modification eliminates the obligation to return or destroy confidential materials upon termination, and it also negates the survival clause for confidentiality, thus allowing the recipient to disclose confidential information post-termination, directly conflicting with standard NDA practices and potentially violating trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing the Recipient to retain confidential materials and eliminating the survival clause for confidentiality, the modified text contradicts the Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act (WUTSA), RCW 19.108, which protects trade secrets. Disclosing or using a trade secret after the agreement terminates, without consent, could constitute misappropriation under WUTSA if the information meets the definition of a trade secret.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Chapter 19.108 RCW: UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 Complete Chapter HTML PDF | RCW Dispositions Chapter 19.108 RCW UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Sections HTML PDF 19.108.010 Definitions. HTML PDF 19.108.020 Remedies for misappropriation — Injunction, royalty. HTML PDF 19.108.030 Remedies for misappropriation — Damages. HTML PDF 19.108.040 Award of attorney's fees. HTML PDF 19.108.050 Court orders to preserve secrecy of alleged trade secrets. HTML PDF 19.108.060 Actions for misappropriation — Time limitation. HTML PDF 19.108.900 Effect of chapter on other law. HTML PDF 19.108.910 Construction of uniform act. HTML PDF 19.108.920 Short title. HTML PDF 19.108.930 Effective date — Application — 1981 c 286. NOTES: Requiring assignment of employee's rights to inventions: RCW 49.44.140 , 49.44.150 .
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text directly contradicts the protection offered by trade secret laws, specifically the Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act (WUTSA), as explained in the Law Explanation. By allowing the Recipient to retain confidential materials and eliminating the survival clause, the modified text permits actions that could constitute misappropriation under WUTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly references Chapter 19.108 RCW, the UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT, indicating the legal framework for trade secret protection in Washington. The change directly undermines the intent of the law to protect trade secrets by allowing their potential misuse after the agreement terminates.
